regions: Corn Belt: IA, IL, IN, OH, MO
Great Plains: KS, NE, SD, ND

what_they_did.daily_work: Operated a diversified family farm, typically 80-320 acres, producing a mix of grain crops (corn, wheat, oats) and livestock (hogs, cattle, poultry, dairy). Unlike Southern monoculture farming, Midwest general farmers practiced crop rotation and mixed husbandry. Daily chores included feeding and watering livestock, milking cows (twice daily), mending fences, and maintaining equipment. Field work consumed most daylight hours during planting and harvest.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Spring (March-May): plowing, disking, planting corn and oats, farrowing hogs. Summer (June-August): cultivating row crops, making hay, threshing small grains. Fall (September-November): corn harvest, filling silos, butchering hogs, shipping cattle. Winter (December-February): livestock care, equipment repair, timber cutting, hauling manure.
tools_and_equipment: Horse-drawn (later tractor-drawn) plow, disk, and harrow
what_they_did.typical_hours: Dawn to dark year-round — 12-16 hours during planting and harvest, 8-10 hours in winter. Livestock required attention 365 days a year regardless of weather or illness.

economics.typical_arrangement: Owner-operators sold crops and livestock at market prices, paid expenses for seed, feed, equipment, taxes, and mortgage, and kept the remainder. Income was highly variable depending on weather, commodity prices, and farm size. Many farmers supplemented income with off-farm labor — carpentry, road work, or winter logging.
economics.annual_income_range: 1900-1910: $500-$1,500/year net farm income depending on acreage and conditions. 1920s farm depression: many farmers earned less than $500/year as commodity prices crashed. 1930s Dust Bowl/Depression: widespread negative income and foreclosures. 1940s-1950s wartime and postwar boom: $2,000-$5,000/year. The 1920 Census of Agriculture reported average farm value in Iowa at $227 per acre — a 160-acre farm was worth $36,000 — but this inflated land value led to crushing debt when prices fell.
economics.comparison: A Midwest farm owner in a good year could earn as much as a factory worker, plus the non-cash value of food produced for the family. In a bad year, the farmer might earn nothing or go deeper into debt. The absence of a steady paycheck was both the freedom and the peril of farming.
economics.debt_cycle: Mortgage debt was the central economic vulnerability. Farmers borrowed to buy land (especially during the WWI boom), then faced foreclosure when commodity prices collapsed in the 1920s-1930s. Between 1930 and 1935, one in four Iowa farms changed hands through forced sales or foreclosure.

hazards: Farm equipment injuries — power take-off (PTO) entanglement, tractor rollovers, corn picker amputations
Livestock injuries from kicks, goring, and crushing
Grain bin suffocation from engulfment in flowing grain
Exposure to extreme cold and heat
Isolation and resulting mental health challenges
working_conditions.living_conditions: Midwest farmhouses varied from modest 4-room frame houses to substantial two-story homes, depending on farm prosperity. Electrification came late to rural areas — only 10% of US farms had electricity in 1930. The Rural Electrification Administration (1935) gradually brought power, but many farms lacked indoor plumbing into the 1950s. Roads were unpaved, and winter isolation could be extreme.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Farming was a family enterprise. Wives managed poultry, dairy, gardens, and household food preservation — these were not sidelines but essential economic contributions. Children worked from age 8-10, progressing from egg gathering to full field work by their teens. The phrase 'family farm' was literal — the labor of every family member was required.
working_conditions.autonomy: High. Owner-operators made their own decisions about what to plant, when to sell, and how to manage their land. This independence was prized and fiercely defended — it was central to the agrarian ideal of Jeffersonian democracy. Tenant farmers had less autonomy but still more than factory workers.

push_factors: Agricultural depression of the 1920s — crop prices fell by 50% after WWI while costs remained high
Dust Bowl and drought (1930s) devastated Plains farmers
Mortgage foreclosures — 'penny auctions' became acts of communal resistance
Mechanization required capital that small farmers couldn't raise
Farm consolidation — 'get big or get out' became the postwar reality
Young people's desire for urban opportunities and education
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Large-scale mechanized agriculture. The number of US farms fell from 6.8 million in 1935 to 2.2 million by 1974. Average farm size roughly tripled. Tractors replaced horses (the tractor population exceeded the horse population on American farms by 1955), combines replaced threshing crews, and chemical inputs replaced crop rotation. The family farm did not disappear, but it became much larger and far more capital-intensive.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: The farm that {ancestor_name} worked in {county} probably looked like it would last forever — {ancestor_name}'s parents had likely worked the same ground. But somewhere around {year}, the math stopped working, and the decision to leave was really just arithmetic.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Farm size, improved and unimproved acreage, crops produced (with quantities), livestock owned, value of farm products, value of implements, farm tenure status
records_generated.where_to_find: National Archives (NARA), FamilySearch microfilm, state archives. The 1850-1880 agricultural schedules are the most detailed, listing individual farm production.
records_generated.research_tip: The agricultural schedule is separate from the population schedule. For ancestor farmers, always check both. The ag schedule reveals the economic profile of the farm in extraordinary detail.

records_generated.record_type: County land records (deeds, mortgages, plat maps)
records_generated.what_it_shows: Land purchases, sales, mortgage amounts, legal descriptions, names of buyers and sellers
records_generated.where_to_find: County recorder of deeds offices, state archives, BLM General Land Office records (glorecords.blm.gov) for original land patents
records_generated.research_tip: The chain of title for a family farm can tell the entire economic story — when they bought, how much they borrowed, when they lost or sold the land. Mortgage records are especially telling.

records_generated.record_type: Farm Security Administration / Farmers Home Administration loan records
records_generated.what_it_shows: Loan applications with detailed family financial information, farm plans, household inventories, photographs
records_generated.where_to_find: National Archives (NARA) Record Group 96, regional NARA facilities
records_generated.research_tip: FSA loan applications from the 1930s-1940s are among the most detailed family economic portraits available anywhere — income, expenses, debts, even what furniture the family owned

census_clues.what_to_look_for: The 1900-1940 censuses distinguish between farm owners (O or Own), renters/tenants (R or Rent), and farm laborers. Check the 'Home owned or rented' column. A farmer who owns free and clear is in a different economic position than one with a mortgage. The agricultural schedule (when available) provides the economic details.
census_clues.red_flags: A family that shifts from 'Owner' to 'Tenant' between census decades likely lost the farm to foreclosure or distress sale — check county land records. A farm family that disappears from a rural Midwest county and reappears in a city (especially during 1920-1940) represents the farm-to-city migration driven by agricultural depression.

cultural_context: The Midwest family farm is the most mythologized occupation in American life — the Jeffersonian ideal of the independent yeoman, self-sufficient and democratic. The reality was more complex: farming was physically punishing, economically precarious, and socially isolating. Yet the farm did produce a distinctive culture — barn raisings, threshing rings, Grange halls, county fairs, and a neighborliness born of mutual dependence. When the farm economy collapsed, it took this social fabric with it. The depopulation of the rural Midwest is one of the defining demographic shifts of 20th-century America.

counter_narratives.claim: Midwest farming was not exclusively white and native-born
counter_narratives.context: Significant communities of German, Scandinavian, Czech, and Dutch immigrants settled the Midwest as farmers. Black farming communities existed in Kansas (Nicodemus), Iowa, and other states, though they faced discrimination in land sales and credit. By the early 20th century, Japanese American truck farmers operated in several Midwest states before internment disrupted their communities.

counter_narratives.claim: Not all farmers who left the land were failures
counter_narratives.context: Some farm families sold at peak land values (especially before the 1920s crash or during the WWII boom) and used the proceeds to start businesses, fund education, or invest in urban real estate. The decision to leave farming was sometimes a calculated choice to capture accumulated land equity, not a forced retreat.

source_references.author: Hurt, R. Douglas
source_references.title: Problems of Plenty: The American Farmer in the Twentieth Century
source_references.notes: Comprehensive survey of the economic and political forces that shaped Midwest farming across the 20th century

source_references.author: Neth, Mary
source_references.title: Preserving the Family Farm: Women, Community, and the Foundations of Agribusiness in the Midwest, 1900-1940
source_references.notes: Essential for understanding women's economic contributions to the family farm and the social networks that sustained rural communities

source_references.author: Stock, Catherine McNicol
source_references.title: Main Street in Crisis: The Great Depression and the Old Middle Class on the Northern Plains
source_references.notes: Documents the devastating impact of the agricultural depression and Dust Bowl on Plains farming communities

source_references.author: Danbom, David B.
source_references.title: Born in the Country: A History of Rural America
source_references.notes: Broad history of rural America with strong coverage of the Midwest farm experience from settlement through modern consolidation
